CURRENT AFFAIRS.

The report of the Land Conference has been issued,
and we find it in many ways an instructive document.
For one thing, it illustrates the difference between a
popular orator addressing a crowd on the atrocities and
injustices of *“ nefarious landlordism,” and the same man
when he sits down to study the practical solution of the
land question with calm consideration of ways and means.
The report is signed by the most distinguished cham-
pions of the tenants’ rights, and it may, therefore, be
taken to record their deliberate opinions of what the
tenants are entitled to.

We pass over the introductory passages of the report;

- they state only what are truisms, on which everyone has

long since made up his mind. That-the system of dual
ownership should be ended, and that it is desirable that
the landlords, when bought out, should continue to live
in’ this country and spend the purchase money of their
estates here are truths which did not require to be enun-
ciated in a series of ponderous paragraphs. Three or four
lines would have been sufficient for the purpose. The
crucial question for everybody concerned is—On what

terms are the landlords to be bought out, wl?a,b number
of years' rent is the tenant to pay for his holding?

To that question the report does not, we regret to find, *
furnish a plain answer. Instead, it beats about the bush.
But the beating process seems to us to issue in results
which are wholly in favour of the landlord. “The pur-
chase price,” we are told, “ should be based upon income ”
—the landlords’ income, we presume—it should involve
“ the payment of a capital sum producing such income at
3 per cent. or 3} per cent.” Let us see how this works
out. We will take the case of a small farmer paying £10
a year rent. The capital sum which invested at 3 or 3%
per cent. would produce this sum would be, roughly,
£300. This would mean thirty years’ purchase of the
rental. This, surely, is not the figure at which the
tenants’ representatives would propose that he should
buy. And if not, what is the price he should pay? And
who is to make up the full capital sum which, invested at
3 per cent., will secure to the landlord his present income?
That is the fundamental question. And on this point the
report leaves us pretty much where we were before it
was issued.

It is true that a saving paragraph is inserted which
demands “ that-the amount of the purchase money pay-
able by the tenant should be extended over a series of
s, and be ab such a rate, in respect of principal and
interest, as will ab once secure a reduction of not less
than 15 per cent. or more than 25 per cent. on second
term rents or their fair equivalent.” This, as it stands,
is a modest dinand on behalf of the tenant. The most it
would secure to our typical tenant would be an abate-
ment of £2 19s. on his present payment; it might, how-
ever, relieve only to the extent of £1 10s. We are
here a long way from the 6s. 8d. in the £1 which the
De Freyne tcnants were encouraged to insist on. And
here that dificrence between the statesman on the plat-
form and the same statesman in council is very forcibly
brought home to us. Further, this report fails to tell us
d:finitely by what means those immediate reductions in
the mants’ present payments are to be brought about,

! that is the essential information which is wanted on
the sibuation.

On the whole, the report is to us a singularly unsatis-
factory documient. It asserts the principle that the land-
lords’ present. income from second term rents must be
secured to him—a principle which will be gratefully en-
dorsed by every landlord in the country. And it lays
down that the tenants must be prepared to accept a re-
duction of 15 per cent. on their present payments—a
principle which the tenants may not find equally pala-
table. But it fails to tell us how these two ends are to be
attained. The solution of the problem it leaves to the
much-denounced British Ministry. We can only hope
that that “ nefarious ” body will be able to deal success-
fully with the conundrum which the Conference has pro-
posed to it.

We recently drew attention to some comments in one
of Mr. Bung's journals in this country, the Grocer and
Vintner. That journal, making what it called a rough
guess, stated that at least one-third of the working
capital of Treland was represented at a recent meeting
of his Imperial Highness, Bung. = Our contemporary, the
great Grocer and Vininer, uncorks a bottle of eloquence
and drowns the LEADER in it. We are “a very abusive
periodical,” though it is acknowledged that we “ occasion-
ally happen to hit the nail on the head.” Ah, there's the
rub! And if the head happens to wear a crown, and if

the crown surrounds the august brow of the Emperor of =

The editor of' the official organ of the Gaelic League 
re·cently expressed the now commonplace view that lite1 a-
ture in the English language, written by Irishmen, is at 
best only Anglo-Irish literature. 
Mr. Seumas 1facManus 
took him ur, and the editor of the official organ printed 
his protest without comment. If au editor advanceo a 
proposition, su1:ely he ought to defend it, or admit that it is 
indefeasible when challenged by a contributor. 
Mr. 
MacManus is, we think, specially futile in his argument. 
Irish literature can be written in English, he contends, 
because Irish soap is Irish though advertised in English. 
It is not Anglo-Irish soap I The logic of this is rather 
staggering. 
Father O'Leary's Seo:on.o., for example, 
would continue to be [rish though it were aclvertisecl in 
Englioh or in French: that is the parallel to Irish soap 
stamped or advertised in English. The industrial parallel 
to •" Irish " literature written and thought out in English 
would be, say, the English-made parts of a bicycle put 
together in Ireland. The bicycle, like the literature, might 
at most be_ called Anglo-Irish. 
Mr. MacManus considers that a man with a knowledge 
of English only can read the literature of Ireland, India, 
the tales of '.Arabia, and soforth. 
Well, if he thinks that, 
of course he thinks it--it is no wonder that he thinks 
further that litei·ature written in English by a m.an who 
knows no Irish, but who was born in Ireland, may be Irish 
-literature. Mr. MacManus does not shine as a logician. 
He argues that -if you translate the original fliacl into 
Irish you sli'.l have Greek literature. 
Nothing of the 
kind. You have an Irish trainslation of Greek literature, 
a.nd nothing<q;1ore, and that is a fundamentally different 
thing from either Irish or Greek literature. Mr MacManus 
promfr'.)S to be aingry should a.nyone, in the event of his 
ever writing lite;ra.ture, call it Anglo-Irish literature--a 
term which he says ·is " as impossible a.s jt js odjous,'' If 
e. Shakespeare were born amongst a French-speaking 
'English-blooded colony in France, and he wrote plays, 
they would stand in the eye of the world as French plays : 
he would have been a creature-as far as one is a creature 
in suoh circumstances-of his French environment, for his 
intellectual food would have been necessarily such as the 
French language offered him ; btit his sympathies might 
naturally go out somewqe.t to the English nation, whose. 
language and literature he did not kn·ow, of whose life and 
traditioqe he only had glimpses; his blood would· also be 
English. 
One might, in the circumstances, reas~ne.bly 
say that his plays in French were not the pure product of 
France; no one could assert that they were the product of 
England ; if anyone objected to his plays being called 
French, they could not reasonably carry' their objection 
farther than to claim them as Anglo-French. 
When 
Mr. MacMauus writes literature in the English language it 
may be English literature, the most it can be is Anglo-
Irish literature. Surely a man born to English, bred on 
English reading-matter, and writing in English is not a 
native Irish thinker- he is not a product of Ireland. 
Anglo-Irish as a term may, in Mr. MacManus' view, be as 
" impossible as it is odious," but when he writes literature 
in the English language, the most it can be described as is 
Anglo-Irish-notwithstanding that it is a term which he 
bolds to be as " impossible as it odious!' 
Iu a review of the Drama for 1902 in the "Irish" Times 
we find under the heading " Home Drama." notices only 
of British plays. 
Of course Britain is really the "home" 
of " the Saved" Irish Times and of its imported editor. 
Under the heading, "The Dublin Stage,'' there is a list of 
some of the imported companies that came here. There 
is no mention of the Irish stage-plays in the Irish 
language. 
Yet, no doubt, in the minds of many who 
think that they are liberal we are bigoted when we say 
that the Protestants, as a body, have their eyes turned 
towards the east where England lies. 
Whoever else may be in the winter of their discontent, 
the Anti-Saurian at least can hardly be. The sun of Sour-
face favour is shining on him with great warmth, and the 
sale of his latest book has thriven accordingly. His 
publishers, whose s,hop is in Grafton-street, inform the 
public by a placar'~1 iu their window that it has now 
reached a third edition, "completing 16,000 copies," 
while his other great work has got to au eighth edition. 
Now we are not going to say anything about the books 
themselves. But there is one thing we must do: we must 
review the condition of those who like them. The Sour. 
faces like them. If they don't, why do they buy the book 
to the extent of 16,000 copies ? Why do they buy another 
of the series to the tune of eight editions? Our friends, 
the Sourfaces of this country, are such a silent, demure 
lot, as a rule, that you have no chance of knowing their 
real sentiments towards you, save in so far as they manifer,t 
them by their actions. It is not a question of what they 
say, but of how they feel ; it is a · question of what 
they leave wnsaid, and of what they silently do or tacitly 
condone. 
But let us figure out this business in a coldly mathema-
tical way. The published price of these books is 7s. 6cl. 
a copy, and the net selling price 6s. 3cl. a copy. 
So the 
window-ticket in the shop says, and, of course, it must be 
right. It also tells us that 16,000 copies of one book, and 
eight editions of the other, have been published. Let us con-
sider these facts. 'l'he average man is an unliterary, non-
bookbuying animal·, aod in his whole life rarely spends 
six-and-threepence ou a book. 
Now, if in a poor country 
like Ireland (of course, we suppose, numbers are sold out 
of Ireland) we find one man's books selling like hot cakes, 
what are we to conclude? Either that the books are of 
exceptional literary merit, or else that there is some uncom-
mon reason for buying them. 
The Anti-Saurian's books 
are not of any literary merit, so that that can't be the 
reason for their big sale ; it must be something ·else. 
What is it? Why do sixteen thousand unliterary, non-
bookbuying animals plank down six-and-threepence each 
for a copy of a book written by au uncommonly common- • 
place man? It must be because they somehow find it 
their account to do so ; because the thing they buy 
answers some appeal of iheir nature, and satisfies some 
secret appetites of their hearts. But the awkward thing 
is that the gratification of this appetite draws atten-
tion to its existence ; the satiation of it to the tune 
of sixteen thousand copies of one book, and eight 
editions of • another, entirely gives away the secret 
hidden so long under .so much demm:eness, so much 
silence, and "non-political" prudence, and the Sourface 
stands revealed as-himself I People don't buy sixteen

- 818

thousand copies of & pelt-the-priests book for justnothing
atall. No; the dirt without evoked a response from the
dirt within, and the Sourface heart rushed to greet the
anti-Catholic book in & way that showed how completely
one is the counterpart of the other. The thing does not
surprise us. It is but an illustration of that most human
of all ‘things, the sincere, the unintentional evidence that
men sometimes give against themselves. Men’s feelings
grow at compound interest when long denied expression,
and the Anti-Saurian’s big sales represent the long-accruing
sourness of the Sourface heart.

" But, to return to our figures. A sale of sixteen
thousand copies of a pelt-the-priests book postulates
sixteen thousand Sourfaces (imncluding the unknown
quantity of the outside of Ireland Sourfaces) with six-
and-threepences to spare. Suppose each of them lends
his mud-book to some few brother Sours, we get easily up
to a reading circulation of fifty thousand. Add to this its
perusal in the family circle, and we get a preuty big
effective total of people who are priest-pelters in their
hearts. The return from the sales of this book equals
exactly £5,000. Did the Sours ever spend that much
money on a book before ? Did they get sixteen thousand
copies of ‘Knocknagow,” for instance? Would they
spend £5,000 on Father Dinneen'’s or Dr. Hyde’s books ?
Not they! Not while a “literary man " like Michael
M‘Carthy appeals to them. But let them go on. So
long as they continue to subsidise priest-pelting and
bishop-baiting, we must continue our campaign, and flog
the badger till he gasps.

We have been requested to publish the following
announcement, :—‘ A preliminary meeting to consider
the formation of a Gaelic League Orchestra will be held
in the rooms of St. Colmcille's Branch of the Gaelic
League, No. 5 Blackhall-street on Friday evening,
January 16th, at eight, p.m., sharp. All Dublin
Branches are cordially invited to attend. Many promi-
nent musicians have already promised to attend and
support the project. Ladies and gentlemen who play
instruments and who are desirous of joining, are re-
quested to communicate with Messrs. Keegan and
Meagher, at above address who are acting as Secretaries
pro tem. The Gaelic League of London has already
decided to form an Orchestra, and it is confidently ex-
pected that Cork, Belfast, Galway, Enniscorthy, Liver-
pool, Manchester, Glasgow, and other great Gaelic
centres will soon follow suit. It is to be hoped a large
number will be present at the meeting to support the
above project.

There was an article recently in the Daily Ezpress on
¢ Irish Masonry in 1902.” We read :—*“ For some years
past the progress of Freemasonry in Ireland has been
uninterrupted, and that notwithstanding the condition of
the country, which at times threatened the peace and good
order of the community. The close of 1902 found the
craft in Iveland more influential and prosperous than
heretofore, and its benevolent objects more extended and
useful than ever. In membership there has been a con-
siderable increase, and during the year six warrants for
new Lodges have heen issued, while only one warrant+has
been called in, and another cancelled. In Dublin, where
there are 39 Lodges of Craft Masonry, 25 Chapters of
Royal Arch Masonry, 11 Preceptories of Knights Templar,
and 7 Chapters of Prince Masons, the year has not .been
a very eventful one, except as regards the extraordinary
success which has made the Masonic Orphan Schools so
conspicuous amongst the educational institutions of the

country.”

the Great Sourface Railway, and tolerant manure manu-
facturer. In face of these two little facts, perhaps it is
no wonder that even the under-housemaid at the Viceregal

In the New - Ireland Review for January, Miss
Katharine Roche makes some observations on our Inter-
mediate schools, which deserve serious attention. She
points out that State aid to education does not consist in
examining the results of the work done in the schools, but
in securing efficient, teaching for them. And she rightly
enough insists that the quality of the teaching depends
primarily on the qualifications of the teacher. In this
respect nothing seems to be done by our Intermediate
Education Board to discharge what ought to be its
primary function. Miss Roche would require from the
schools some assurance that the teachers employed in
them were fitted for their business—a requirement which
can be satisfied only by insisting on some certificate of
competency from everyone teaching in a school to which
public money is paid for educational work. We can
ourselves see no hardship in this demand. But a diffi-
culty presents itself when we ask: Where is this certificate
to be procured? Where are our teachers of Intermediate
schools to receive the training which they now so plenti-
fully lack? It is here that the want of a University for
Catholics makes itself keenly felt. The want affects the
whole system of Secondary Education in the conutry.

‘We must, however, admit that the teachers do not
show themselves particularly anxious to secure the advan-
tages with which Miss Roche would provide them. We
have heard of an excellent Training College for teachers
of Intermediate schools, established and equipped by the
Ursuline Community in Waterford. But our information
leads us to believe that the ladies who are adopting teach-
ing as a profession show little appreciation of the benefits
it offers them, and prefer starting in their career with
such equipment as native wit and an ordinary school
education furnish.

The Independent is publishing a list of industrial firms
that existed in various centrés in 1899. We note that
under the heading “ Dundalk,” there is no mention of
the firm of Messtrs. P. J. Carroll and Co.,  tobacco
manufacturers. It so happens that the tobacco industry
in Dundalk, as represented by Messrs. Carroll and Co., is
a very important affair. At the present time the fim
employs about 90 or 100 hands. An industry that em-
ploys such an amount of labour as that in a comparatively
small town like Dundalk is by no means one to be over-
looked.

The possibilities of the foreign markets are not realised,
we fear, even in the dreams of the average Irish manufac-
turers. The twenty millions, or thereabouts, of the Irish
race abroad are a “ tried and true” myth ; but the people
of Irish birth and Irish parentage abroad who might be
easily turned into customers for Irish goods are a very solid
fact, even though the Irish manufacturers as a body do
not appreciate it. Take the case of Messrs. Carroll, of
Dundalk, as a case in point. When the LEADER was young
they practically had no Scotch trade: they are now about
opening a depot in Glasgow for the accommodation of
their trade in Scotland. At the present time, the number
of hands they employ is considerably larger than the
number they employed when the LEADER started, and
their view is that they will put on fifty per cent. to
their present number within six months if things keep
forging ahead as they are now doing. Messrs. Carroll
and Co. have now a very strong and a growing business
connection in Scotland. They put it down fairly and
squarely to the influence of the LEADER. The LEADER has
benefitted many. firms that hate it, and who would, we

THE GAELIC LEAGUE ORGAN AND THE BREHON
LAWS.

N the current issue of An Claidheamh Soluis nearly
four full columns of small print are occupied in
proving Dr. Atkinson’s Irish scholarship to be defective.
From the previous issue of the same paper we learn that
the court of investigation is only being opened, and are
threatened with much more of the same kind of * proof.”
As one who expects a reasonable pennyworth in the shape
of a paper I protest in the strongest manner possible
against any further such “proofs’ of Dr. Atkinson’s
ignorance of Irish. How is the demonstration worked
out? The four eolumns I spoke of consist of letters to
the Academy of the year 1885 from Whitley Stokes,
Norman Moore, Standish H. O'Grady, etc., pointing out
errors in the text of the then published volumes of the
Brehon Laws. In charity to its readers the Claidheamh
abridges the letters, and says: “ We merely wish to give
our readers an idea of what scholars thought of Dr.
Atkinson’s work then.” WNow, the only volume of the
Brehon Laws edited by Dr. Atkinson bears the date 1901,
which is the date also of his Glossary to the entire work
of which we have heard so much. How errors in previous
volumes, pointed out in 1885, can prove the ignorance of
the editor of a volume published in 1901 is more than T
can see. The four columns of letters I know prove up
to the hilt the ignorance—the crass, hopeless ignorance—of
an editor other than that of the Brehon Laws. The organ
of the Gaelic League, which found not a line of space to
refer to an interesting lecture on O’Cwurry—to whom,
beyond all others, the possibility of publishing the Brehon
Laws at all is due—recently delivered in Dublin, now
devotes pages week after week to prove that he was a
myth ; and that Atkinson, and Atkinson only, is the trans-
lator and editor of the Brehon Laws. The story of the
editing of the Brehon Laws is a long and tragic story and
would furnish a subject for an interesting drama. The
treatment of poor O'Curry, as is well known, was shame-
ful, but is nothing compared to the wrong done him by
the organ of the League. in giving all the glory to
Dr. Atkinson.

CATHOLICS AND OPEN COMPETITION.

N a recent article I set forth; I think, with fairness,
the prospects of Catholics who .go through the
University. 1 incidentally pointed out the rather blank
‘outlook which awaits them on emerging from their studies
unless they have very considerable capital to assist them.
The difficulty is, no doubt, in part a phase of that general
proposition so well expressed by your contributor in the
formula, “No Industry, No Anything,” and in so far as
it is, i3 not capable of a separate remedy. If we had
industries, we would require a constant supply of sclenhfm
students (¢.g., chemists) to minister to the intellectual side
of manufacture.. There would be many new openings in
this direction. But the results would extend even further.
Literature and Art, ever the offspring of Industrial pros-
perity, would call for a still further supply of educated
men. So far as the difficulty is due to such economic
causes we must look to an economic remedy. But the
fact that there is really a demand for educated Catholics,
especially for executive posts, shows that Industrial short-
comings are not the sole reason. There actually exisb
many positions which ought to be filled by University
Catholics, but which they are somehow artificially prevented
from attaining. There is a demand which is probably
sufficient to absorb all the Catholics at present produced,
but, as I showed in my former article,the supply seems
never to meet if.

What, then, is the remedy, in so far as the evil admits of
being specially dealt with? How are we to secure to Catho-
lics a fair share of the Governmental and other posts which
ave, roughly speaking, reserved for men with a higher
education. Ifsome body such as the Catholic Association
wishes to attain fair play for our co-religionists, how is it
to proceed ? ‘“ Protest’” seems at first sight the most
ready means, a protest renewed as each place is filled up,
and backed up by the influence of such Catholics as are in
power at present. Such measures might, of course, do
some good. Yet it is hard to exercise any constant vigi-
‘lance over Government departments. It requires a keen,
scientific perception to watch the changing phases of job-
bery, to say when the unqualified becomes the temporary,
and at what precise moment the temporary changes into the
permanent official. Moreover, it is only to the more pro-
minent post that such a supervision. could apply, and
when it was objected that a non-Catholic head of a
department had once again been appointed, it would,
perhaps, be answered with truth, that there was no
Catholic qualified to assume the post. Seeing also that
Mr. Morley found it hard to get Catholics to fill offices,
even a Government favourable to our claims might find it
difficult to get suitable men.

It is, indeed, rather at the sbottom than at the top that
such a reform must commence. If you are to promote
Catholies to important posts, you must have Catholics in
minor posts from which they may be advanced. It is
just in this mafter of producing’ a reguiar graduated
system that the method of repeated protiests or continuous
vigilance: would io:il. Moreover, the fate meted out to
Mr. Plunkett by his fellow Conservatives is scarcely likely
to encourage heads of departments to listen favourably
to Catholic protests. I would venture to suggest
that, if any reform from the Catholic point of view is to
be worked in the distribution of patronage, it must come
not from \yorkmg on the conscience of the appointers or
the executive, but from wholly changing the method of
appointment. As long as the present executive system -

prevails in Ireland, if all the better posts in the public
. seryice continue to be given by influence, Catholics can
never hope to have a look in. " Protests avail very little

A LECTURE ON REPTILES.

M\ ENTLEMEN,—In the course of this lecture it is
not my intention to discuss the whole of the great
class Reptilia. Such a task could not be accomplished in
one short lecture, besides it would be quite unnecessary.
I shall deal with only a small, but peculiar, group, which,
in order to avoid technical terms, I shall refer to by its
popular or trivial name The Truelie. Up to some three
years ago this group was not included in the Reptilia at
all, but constituted a sus generss or family in itself. During
that short interval, however, the study of these creatures
bas been taken up on scientific lines, with the result that
nearly all our previous notions regarding their functions
and life history have been scattered to the winds. Most,
if not all, of the work accomplished in this direction has
been done by the LrapEr school of investigators. The
method adopted in this school, as far as I can understand,
seems to be, in the first place, the accumulation of facts by
observation and experiment, and secondly, the discussion
of these facts in accordance with the rules of logical
thought. In recommendation of this method I need say
nothing : the results which it has achieved in this, as well
as in other departments of research, are sufficient testimony
of its excellence.

I have here four specimens of the Truelie group, three
of which have been taken in the county Dublin, and for
the other I have to thank the proprietors of the ‘“ Only
Raimeis Factory” in Cork. They resemble one another
so closely in some points that they may be discussed
together in a general way in the first instance before
taking up the more detalled examination of each. On
holding any of these up to the light you notice, at once,
its peculiar green colour. At one time it was supposed
that this particular colour was an unerring indication of
health, and as these reptiles were, and still are largely
consumed by ‘the natives, the greener varieties became
immensely popular. Now, on the Darwinian principles,
it follows that because greenery was an advantage in the
struggle for existence it would develop more and more.
This was found to be the case till about three years ago,
when there was evidence of a decided change in the publio
taste, and, in fact, in the whole atmosphere of the country.
In the history of these reptiles there is nothing more
marvellous than the fact that, while they seem to be
quite unconscious of any change, they have, one and all,
set about adapting themselves fo it. This must be a very
painful process, during which the poor creatures are almost
certain to lose the whole of their present colour, and other
distinguishing characteristics, if they do not actually become
extinet. The next point to which I shall have to draw
your attention is the rudimentary formation of the back-
bone in all four species. No satisfactory explanation of
this has yet been brought forward, but personally I am
inclined to the belief that it came about in this way.
Suppose a normally developed backbone in the first
instance. Then i is easy to understand how crawling
habits and uncontrolled curiosity would take the creature
frequently into awkward situations from which it would
be difficult or impossible to escape without a high degree
of flexibility. The presence of anything rigid would be on
such occasions not only useless, but might prove fatal,
and natuve, with a view to fitting the creature to its en-
vironments would, therefore, reduce the offending member
to its present condition. Before proceeding to the con-
sideration of their peculiarities I shall touch on one
other feature common to all the .members of the
Truelie family, and that is the extraordinary en-
largement of the 7aimeis gland, especially in the
greener members. Prior to the LEADER epoch, it was
a popular belief—as it still seems to bs in many remote
districts—that this ruimeis was a sovereign remedy for

The first with which I propose to deal is the Tempes Cro-
codilem—a native of Dublin It is well known that it is
violently agitated by the sight of moral depravity in any
form. So sensitive had it become in this respect, that
recently a large dose of Behting Komishon had to be
administered subcutancously. The operation was an entire
success, and it is not expected that there will be a repeti-
tion of the previous symptoms. In the tissues of the
Tempes there is to be found a most pernicious parasite
known as the Tutowan Nafeul or the Tutowan Bahwan.
It is not very dependent on its host for subsistence, but
feeds on various organisms, including another parasite,
namely, the Heleva Vella. Although the mischief wrought
by these parasites, in other respects, is dreadful to con-
template their presence is undoubtedly beneficial to the
circulation. The only other peculiarity I shall notice is
the development of its Nuez area which, although more
apparent than real, is yet accountable for the popularity of
. the Tempes in some quarters. The next specimen is the
well-known Bycotus Pyus, also a resident of Dublin. If
is not by any means an old reptile, having emerged out of
the Great Split only some years ago.

Morally it is more highly developed than the Tempes, a
fact already suggested by its name. Its chief claim to
notice, however, is in connection with its movements.
These are, on the whole, very spasmodic, and are con-
trolled partly by what is called the musculus De
Taloni or the Tallon muscle, so named after the illustrious
man who first discovered its use. There is nothing else
in the Bycotus worthy of attention except, perhaps, its
Noetz which are said to be the staple food of certain low
grades of society. The third specimen is the Craw-
thumpa Vulgaris, found in the same regions as those
previously discussed. ~ This is, perhaps, the oldest
‘member of the Truelie family, but notwithstanding its
* great-age it still retains much of its youthful activity.
During a single night it has been known to perform the
gigantic feat of crawling from the level of Sapho to the
summit of Pastoral. This is accomplished by means of
the Skala Coppa or powerful claws with which the reptile
is furnished. Authorities are not agreed as to which of
the three foregoing forms possesses the greatest advantage
in the struggle for existence. I can think of no more
satisfactory way of deciding the question than by confining
the trio in'a small cage and carefully observing the result.
I have placed the Munster specimen last in the series, not
because it is less deserving than the others, but rather for
the contrary reason. It is described under yarious names ;
‘the one which I select—namely, Comicus Putridus,
will be found as suitable as any other. The whole
condition of this reptile is such as to fill one with a mix-
ture of pity and disgust. It is incapable of moving in
any direction except under the i ntuence of some powerful
stimulant, and even then it goes_ubnut in a da:zed and
 stupid fashion. On dissecting the tissues we find immense
- quantities of foreign matter, and the sensational paragraphs
are usually abnormally developed. Going a little deeper
we come upon the Jocoze, a sort of compound organ,
~ which is in great demand as an article of diet, and to
" which even members of the judicial beqch are known to
be partial. In the case of another reptile, which became

~ extinct in Munster a few years ago, it is a significant fact

. If in this lecture I have failed to add anything to your
information. let me hope, at least, that I have stimulated

A BENCH OF BUNG MAKERS AT KILMAINHAM.

HE_ Court was crowded. It was not the usual seedy,

l disreputable-looking crowd of the Police Court,

- buta most “ respectable " crowd, There were no “ drunks "
to be tried, no wife-beaters to be charged, no parents to be
sentenced for neglecting their children. No. It was a
most “ respectable ’ crowd. As for the Bench, it was
pno_ked. The magistrates had not room to turn round,
Quite a num})er had to stand, whilst others were forced to
find a place in the gallery. One, at least, got separated
in the crush from his brothers, and, being isolated, was
t0o shy to vote when the Recorder called for ** hands up.”
But it didn’t madtter.

The occasion was the opening day of the Kilmainham
Quarter Sessions, and a large number of Mr. Bungs and
Mrs. Bungs were applying for transfers of licences and
for new licences in respect of different public-houses in
the county Dublin. Sitting on a corner of a bench, and
surrounded by an atmosphere of such eminent “ respecta-
bility,” I watchead the proceedings, at first with interest,
then with amazement, and finally with feelings of disgust
and contempt. It was a great day for Bung, for, though
nominally on trial, Bung had nothing to fear, and won all
along the line. Application followed application, now for
a transfer, now for a new licence, and always with the
same result. Not a single one was refused. One only
was adjourned, because, fortunately, the Court had no
jurisdiction to deal with it. The rest were granted.

The thing was a ridiculous farce from start to finish,
and if it can be taken as typical of such performances—
which there is no reason to doubt—proves the rottenness
of the whole system of licensing control as it exists at
present. It proves, moreover, that even if the system can
be justified on any grounds, its organisation is nothing
short of a scandal and a menace to the public welfare,
because it is quite clear from the proceedings at Kilmain-

- ham on Tuesday week, that the great majority of the
magistrates who acted on the Bench were totally unfitted
for the proper discharge of the most important functions
of their office. This, however, is only the natural result
of the system of organisation, under which men are ap-
pointed to the magistracy by favour and social influence,
and without any regard for their fitness or unfitness to act
as the judges in masfters so vitally important to the public
interest as those which came before them at Kilmainham.

In the course of the sitting the  Recorder remarked that
““there was a rather favourable Bench for the publicans.”
It was a mild way of hinting at the truth, but several
magistrates, with a righteous indignation worthy of Bung
itself, protested against the Recorder’s remark. Poor,
misguided J.P.’s! What an injustice to insinuate that
they were prejudiced in favour of “the trade” and the
would-be publicans. Why, they only granted cvery
application ; and one of them, Sir George Moyers, whoever
he is, was evidently so overcome with sorrow, that the
Bench could not do more for ¢ the trade,” that he actually
said that one applicant, who applied only for a beer
licence, ‘¢ ought to get a_spirit licence as well.” What
more than that could the Temperance Party desire. Then
to see how fiercely and nobly the police fought for tem-

applicant, and in others on the grounds of the existiug
drinking accommodation being more than enough. Their
evidence? Evidence indeed !—what do the police know:
about evidence? They produced none. Their legal
representative, a Mr. Liynch, simply bobbed up from his
_seat on each ocoasion, and said :—“I oppose, my Lord,
on behalf of the police.” That was evidently meant to be
crushing, for he never interfered further. Indeed, even
e most casual observer, must have been amazed at the
cical * opposition " of the police.

 refer to the individual cases that came before the
t would be impossible here, but mention may be made
gé or two. Most of the applications came from
=

THE LEADER. 323

; visibing any officsr concerned —if such should be discovered

Kingstown, where, by the way, owing to the action of the
lords of the soil, the already enormous drinking facilities
have been more than doubled because of the older and
smaller drunkeries having been pulled down, and gorgeous
gin palaces erected in their stead. In one case the appli-
cant confessed that out of £1,300 purchase-money paid for
a public-house, the National Bank advanced the astound-
ing sum of £950. In another case the Ulster Bank
lent the money. This pernicious practice, indulged in by
the banks, has grown into a scandalous abuse, and it is high
time that steps were taken to put a check to it. Of course
the magistrates didn’t care—they granted the applications
without a murmur; but what about the shareholders in
the banks? Would not many of these pious people be
shocked to learn that they are in reality drunkery owners,
often of the worst description? Yet they ave, for the
banks practically own an enormous number of drink shops,
and the unfortunate grocer's curate, who puts his few
scraped-together guineas into a drunkery, and raises the
rest of the money required from the bank, knows that he
will be turned out on the roadside without a penny piece
unless he is able to meet the demands of the bank punc-
tually. He can’t afford to be particnlar as to the trade
he does—and he isn’t.

Another case was that of a man who had bought a
public-house that actually encroaches on what should be
the grounds of the handsome Catholic Church at Kings-
town, and utterly spoils its appearance, to say nothing of
its objectionable proximity to the house of God. The
priests did not oppose the application, though the late
respected Dean Walsh spent a large sum in buying up
and pulling down a handsome private residence that stood
at another corner of the church. It was left for two
Protestant clergymen to go into the box against it, and for
the Protestant Recorder to say, *“ that he was not a member
of the Catholic commuuity, but, if he were, he would
strongly object to a great flaming public-house, on which
£2,500 was to be spent, being erected at the entrance to
that beautiful church of divine worship at Kingstown."
Several Catholic magistrates sat in silence upon the Bench.
Did they oppose it? Not they, even though on the merits
of the case the Recorder urged the Bench most forcibly to
reject the application. Needless to say, it was granted,
as also another new licence on the other side of the road,
ten yards away.

These are but one or two instances out of a long list.
Each application, as it came up, seemed to be granted by
the magistrates for the sole reason that its rejection would
disappoint and inconvenience the applicant. The respon-
sibility of increasing the already enormous number of
drunkeries—each of them a temptation in itself—did not
weigh with them for a moment. The public good, the
regeneration of the people, was not considered at all.
The old policy of “oblige” was over it all. And.yet some
of these same County Magistrates will hold up their hands
in pious horror when, later on in another court, some poor
drunken, degraded girl is dragged before them to receive
¢ justice " at their hands. Will they remember then that
it was they who increased the temptation for the unhappy
girl, and that it is they who are partly responsible for her
ruin? They will not. But their responsibility before God
will remain as surely as poverty and ruin, and sin and
crime will follow as the direct result of their action at
Kilmainham.

And so the proceedings were not all farce after all.
They had their element of tragedy.

to Euston station noticed a little girl sitting on the kerb 
crying. On asking the .cause of her trouble he was told 
the astounding story that the previous day she had been 
sent by the nuns from a convent at Castlerea to a Protes-
tant gentleman named Lake in London, who was to meet 
her at Euston station. 
She had, it appears, a ticket put 
in her hand at Castlerea station and sent to London to be 
met by an English Protestant gentleman, and on her 
arrival at Euston without a single farthing of money in 
her pocket aucl no person to meet her had to walk about 
the streets cold and starving. 
"The kind-heartecl policeman brought her to his home, 
where his wife made her comfortable and gave her a bed, 
and this morning she was taken to a convent. 
" This much of the story is absolutely true, but what 
there is underlying the whole I have yet to learn." 
This, certainly, struck us as an extraordinary story'; 
but before we had set any enquiries on foot we received a 
further letter from Mr. Walsh. It will be remembered 
that the girl was taken to a convent in London. Writ-
ing two days afterwards, December 30th, he enclosed us 
a letter from St. Edward's Convent of Our Lady of 
Mercy, 11 Harewood-avenue, London, N.vV. 
In that 
letter the writer, one of the Sisters of the Convent, 
thanks Mr. Walsh for his kindness to the little Irish girl. 
She states that Mr. Lake, the gentleman with whose 
family the girl was to have taken service, called to see 
the girl, and that the girl decided not to go with him as 
there were six in family and washing to be done : she 
felt that she was unequal to the work. The nun, there-
fore, sent a woman with the girl to Euston. This woman 
saw her safely quartered in the train and booked her 
through to Castlerea. It appears from the letter that 
Mr. Walsh had kindly given £2 out of his own pocket to 
defray expenses. The nun stated that the girl ought to 
have arrived at Euston on Saturday morning, but missed 
her boat, and that from that mishap the confusion re-
sulted. She also states that the nuns at Castlerea have 
another young girl as " mother's help " with Mrs. Lake's 
sister, and that it was this young woman who asked the 
nuns to send the girl to Mrs. Lake. The nun adds that 
Mr.: Lake was disappointed, but exceedingly kind to the 
girl. 
The fare to Castlerea was £1 13s. 8cl. ; cab to 
Euston, ls. llcl.; cab fare in Dublin, 2s.; total, £117s. 7d. ; 
and the nun returned the balance of 2s. 5cl. to Mr. Walsh. 
All's well that ends well; but, all the same, this is an 
extraordinary case. 
Does this convent think that it 
serves Ireland by sending an Irish girl to London, where 
vice abounds, to be servant to a family of six, where 
washing is to be done? What might have become of the 
girl if the kind-hearted policeman and Mr. Walsh had 
not met with her. The girl lost her boat, it is true; but 
why was not that possibility guarded against or provided 
for ? Of course, the nuns of Castlerea thought they were 
doing the best for the girl. But nuns can know little of 
the world; why, then, take the responsibility of sending 
this poor girl on her way to London, to be a servant to a 
fo,mily of six, where washing was to be done ; send her 
on a journey in the course of which, as it turned out, she 
lost her boat and was eventually found by a policeman, 
crying on a London kerb ? 
One of the facts of this country is that the people of it 
generally do not "know their way about." Fancy, in 
these times, when prelates, priests, and laymen are 
interested in the awful emigration question, a Catholic 
convent in Loughrea., in face of the fact that servants are 
difficult to procure in Ireland, exporting a girl to London,

sc~ool~ ~ring up their scholars as if they were English

"said nothing which is really in conflict" with anything

As to my purpose in writing, I see no use, but rather

brought up here in Ireland,'" the Irish Party would be 
able to show that the schools are not Irish, and that, there-
fore, arguments based on that assumption had no force. 
It is, I think, far better that we should say the simple 
truth with regard to those schools. 
Over-statements 
damage even a good case in the end. 
111op labours, somewhat unnecessarily, I think, my 
statement that the nuns who taught a certain school were 
"very English." I did not say.that as if blaming them; 
it is to their credit that, being English, they were very 
English. 
I simply wanted to show that, being very 
English, they must inevitably have an Anglicising influence 
on the children they taught

not guess why I criticised him, he guessed that the school

London, and 90 per cent. of its scholars bore Irish names. 
Were they likely, in that school, to be brought up "as 
Irish as if they were here in Ireland"_? I was a scholar 
there and I know they were not. They were, as I said 
before, as Irish as their home atmosphere made them. 
The schools, so far as they exercised influence, ~xercised 
it in a;n Anglicising direction.

The best point mop makes is that I can only give a few

each in poor districts with the Irish element predominating 
among the Catholics. Moreover, I knew in later life, and 
know still, many of the products of those schools. Generally 
speaking, they are not " as Irish as if they were brought 
up in Ireland." Indeed I do not see how one could expect 
that they would be, nor can I underst_and how anyone 
with much knowledge . of them could seriously make such 
a statement. mor says he ~ight cancel or contradict

think he had better do so.

It 1s, but on all

Anglicising when taught in Ireland, with all t~J.8 corrective 
influences of Irish life and character and tradition around, 
how much more so when taught in English schools by 
English persons who are enthusiastic about their history? 
Apropos of enthusiasts, I well remember the zest and 
spirit with which those English nuns taught the small 
Irish-Cockneys to sing a song which ran somewhat 
thus -
" I am a British boy, sir, 
I joy to tell it you." 
An Irish father objected, and the song was dropped, 
but there were strained relations between the objector's 
family and the good nuns for long after. 
Another reason I had for writing was that 111011's 
statement, if allowed to go unchallenged, would help to 
bolster up that old myth about Irish nationality persisting 
indefinitely in the descendants of the Irish beyond sea. 
It is a worn old myth, and 'tis nearly time it died. 
The 
descendants of the Irish in England ultimately become 
English, and the schools help in making them so. It is, 
I suppose, natural and inevitable. All healthily organised 
countries gradually absorb and assimilate the people who 
come into and remain in them. Remembering the relations 
of the two races it is a painful thought that the vast 
majority of the descendants of the Irish in England will 
become English. There is, however, no use in shutting 
one's eyes to it, or in hugging to oneself the pleasing 
delusion th(t.t, in our case, natural laws become inoperative, 
and that the Irish in England remain forever Irish. 
There is no lasting "Greater Ireland," unless it be in 
Heaven, for, without continued emigration from Ireland, 
the Irish in England and· America-who make up now 
what is sometimes called " Greater Ireland "-must 
inevitably become as completely merged in the people of 
those countries as are the descendants of " the Wild 
Geese " in the Continental nations. The Catholic schools 
help the process of merging here in England, and, after 
my years of experience of them, I could not allow to pass 
uncontradicted the statement that " the children in them 
are as Irish as if they were brought up here in Ireland."

B was from my own place, and quite new to London,
new also in that branch of the Civil Ser-

seen twenty years of service, London was an open

was a genuine pleasure to take him round on
occasional half-day sight-seeing journeys. We were going
home late one evening after such a half-day when our
tram stopped for a moment near the Jewish Cemetery.
I think he had a little deeper tinge ‘than usual of our
national melancholy, and the sight of the graveyard at
once arrested his attention. In any case it is a place one
would hardly pass unheeding. The square tree-bordered
space, strewn with flat stone-covered graves and green
with long rank grass, seems strangely out of keeping with
the roarng blatant East End life that surges forever past
it. The high old-fashioned wall and the little synagogue
within, give the place an atmosphere of aloofness, of dark
reserve, and at all times there is something of pathos in
its aspect. One gets the “sense of tears in human things”
as one thinks of the dead Hebrews resting there after God
knows how much wandering. It is a perpetual reminder
of the strange destiny of that unique race, perseouted and
powerful, having its home in all lands and belonging to
none.  On this quiet moonlit night, with the soft tremu-
lous rustle of the leaves alone to break the silence, and
with the grey glimmer of the flat tombstones and the
sheen of the moonlight on the long grass, it was indeed
place to set one brooding. ? ;

He was a little more subdued now, but he would have
none of my reasoning. He protested that he would marry
10 one but an Irish girl, though marriage was far enough
from his thoughts. ~ And he wouid get back to Ireland
as soon as a chance offered. “T hate this place and I hate
the people,” said he, “and Tll never settlo among them
and grow like some of them I see.” I winced a little at
the scorn in his tone, though it was hardly intended for
me. When the tram reached its destination, and we set
out to walk the remainder of our journey, the talk be-
tween us was a little constrained, and both, I think, were
rather relieved when we said “ Good-night.”

Not many months later I went from London on pro-
motion. It was a matter of regret to me, for in London
there are Irish colonies in which something of an Irish
atmosphere, attenuated though it be, is maintained,
whereas in many of the lesser towns an Irishman is lost.
He is “a stranger in a strange land ” to an extent thaf
Irishmen who know only great centres such as London,
Liverpool and Glasgow can hardly realisc.  The Irish
weekly papers, his few books, and his memories are al-
most the only things that keep him in touch with Ireland
at all; especially if, as in my case, he has a
large family and cannot afford to go home on
holiday. In my new home I heard news from
London  occasionally. My young friend, O'Reilly,
I heard of as an active politician for a year or so.
Then came eclipse, for like myself he went from
Lendon to a distant seaport. Later on I learned from
the chance talk of a colleague that he had gone back ta
London again.

Fully twelve years elapsed before I set foot in the great
metropolis again, and then the occasion was a sad one.
An old and dear friend of mine had gone the way of all
